era_context: Late Bronze Age. The Hittite Empire under Šuppiluliuma I is at or near its peak of territorial expansion. Egypt is in the 18th Dynasty, a period of imperial power and cultural achievement. Mycenaean Greece is developing its palace civilization. Babylon is under Kassite rule. The Shang Dynasty continues in China. This is a period of complex international relations, with multiple powers competing for influence and resources across the Eastern Mediterranean and Near East.
geographic_coverage_gaps: South Asia (limited written records for this period)

events.title: Šuppiluliuma I's consolidation of Hittite power in Anatolia
events.description: Šuppiluliuma I continues to consolidate Hittite control over Anatolia, suppressing internal rebellions and establishing a centralized administrative system. The Hittite state develops a sophisticated bureaucracy, legal codes (the Hittite Laws), and military organization. This period sees the establishment of vassal relationships with neighboring kingdoms and the development of the Hittite Empire as a major regional power.
events.certainty_note: Šuppiluliuma I's reign is well-attested, but precise dating of individual events is uncertain by ±2–3 years.

events.title: Egyptian military campaigns in Nubia and the Levant
events.description: The Egyptian 18th Dynasty continues its imperial expansion, with military campaigns in Nubia to secure gold and other resources, and in the Levant to maintain Egyptian influence over vassal states. These campaigns are part of a broader strategy to establish Egyptian hegemony in the Eastern Mediterranean and Africa. The pharaoh (likely Amenhotep II or Thutmose IV) commands these military operations.

sources.name: Egyptian temple inscriptions and administrative records
Egyptian military records and campaign narratives

events.certainty_note: The general pattern of Egyptian military campaigns is well-established; specific events in this year are inferred from broader historical trends.

events.title: Development of Linear B script in Mycenaean Greece
events.description: Mycenaean scribes are developing or refining the Linear B script, an adaptation of the earlier Linear A script used in Minoan Crete. Linear B is used for administrative and economic record-keeping in Mycenaean palaces. This development reflects the increasing complexity of Mycenaean palace economies and the need for written record-keeping. The script will be fully attested in later palace archives.

sources.name: Archaeological evidence of early Linear B inscriptions
Pottery and other artifacts with Linear B writing

events.certainty_note: The development of Linear B is dated to the Late Helladic II period, but the precise timing is uncertain by ±10–20 years. The assignment of this year to the development of Linear B is conventional.

events.title: Shang oracle bone divination practices
events.description: The Shang Dynasty continues its practice of oracle bone divination, in which the king and his diviners inscribe questions on animal bones and shells, heat them, and interpret the resulting cracks to divine the will of the ancestors and the spirits. These oracle bones provide evidence of Shang religious beliefs, political concerns, and administrative practices. The practice is central to Shang royal authority and decision-making.
events.certainty_note: Oracle bones are contemporary evidence, but their assignment to specific years is uncertain by ±50–100 years due to disputes over Shang chronology.

disconfirming_evidence: The chronology of Šuppiluliuma I's reign is debated. Some scholars place his major consolidation of power earlier (c. 1344–1322 BCE in conventional chronology), while others argue for a lower chronology. The assignment of this year to his consolidation is therefore provisional and may be off by 50–100 years. Additionally, the identity of the reigning Egyptian pharaoh is uncertain due to chronological disputes in Egyptology.
historiographic_note: The record for 1813 BCE is similar to previous years: dominated by Near Eastern and Egyptian sources, with Mycenaean and Chinese evidence from archaeology and inscriptions. The Hittite and Egyptian archives are the most detailed, but these are state-sponsored documents. The record is heavily skewed toward elite political, military, and religious history. Economic structures, social organization, and the lives of common people are underrepresented. Sub-Saharan Africa, the Americas, Oceania, and Central Asia are essentially absent from the written record.

graph_edges.note: Hittite consolidation of power in Anatolia enables expansion into the Levant and conflict with Egypt.
Egyptian and Hittite imperial expansion are concurrent processes in the Eastern Mediterranean.
Mycenaean development is contemporary with Egyptian and Hittite imperial activities.
